questions.scenario: One ECS task crashes during a deploy. The ALB keeps sending checkout requests to two healthy tasks, and customers do not notice. Which resilience idea is this?

options.text: Serving through a small runtime failure by keeping healthy task copies available.
Recovering from data loss, because task replacement restores deleted order rows.
Meeting RPO, because no backup was restored.
Replacing all observability, because the service stayed online.

questions.explanation: Resilience includes serving through small failures. Multiple healthy tasks let checkout continue when one task restarts. That is different from recovering lost or corrupted data.

questions.scenario: A bad write corrupts order totals at 10:12 UTC. The business says checkout must be safe again within 30 minutes and no more than about 5 minutes of accepted order writes can be lost.

options.text: The 30-minute target is RTO, and the 5-minute data-loss target is RPO.
The 30-minute target is RPO, and the 5-minute data-loss target is RTO.
Both targets are the same because recovery time and data freshness mean one thing.
Neither target matters once a database backup exists.

questions.explanation: RTO is about how long recovery can take. RPO is about how fresh recovered data must be, or how much recent data loss is acceptable. A backup setting only matters if it can actually meet those targets.

questions.scenario: Checkout returns 201, but receipt emails are delayed because the `devpolaris-receipt-email` Lambda is failing. The API is usable, but customers do not receive receipts.

options.text: This is degraded service, not necessarily full checkout downtime, and it still deserves an owned recovery path.
This is permanent data loss because the API returned 201.
This proves RTO and RPO are irrelevant for side jobs.
This should be ignored because delayed email can never affect customer trust.

questions.explanation: Resilience depends on user impact. Orders may complete while a side job is degraded, so the urgency and response differ from checkout 500s. The team should still know how to recover the side job and communicate impact.

questions.scenario: Which choices are resilience choices with cost tradeoffs?

options.text: Keeping at least two ECS tasks so one restart does not remove all healthy targets.
Keeping RDS backups and testing restore so bad writes or deletion can be repaired.
Retaining enough logs to diagnose support reports after the original incident window.
Cutting every retained signal and backup because smaller bills automatically mean safer systems.

questions.explanation: Each correct choice has a cost side and a protection side. Running tasks protects availability, backups protect recovery, and logs protect diagnosis. Blindly cutting all protection may reduce cost while increasing business risk.

questions.scenario: A proposal saves money by shortening backup retention until it removes the only tested restore point for orders. The proposal has no updated RTO or RPO evidence.

options.text: Reject or revise it until recovery targets are still believable and tested.
Approve it because cost savings are proof that resilience improved.
Approve it if ECS still has enough CPU, because CPU replaces backups.
Ignore RPO because users only care about application uptime.

questions.explanation: A cost change that breaks the only restore path is not a safe win. Recovery targets need evidence: backup schedule, restore process, validation, app config, and practice. Cost and resilience should be reviewed together.
